guides-01-overview--docs.name: Docs
guides-01-overview--docs.title: Guides/01 Overview
guides-01-overview--docs.content:
import { Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';

<Meta
  title="Guides/01 Overview"
  summary="What Breeze UI v3 provides, how its primitives and patterns fit together, and where to begin."
/>

# Breeze UI

Breeze UI is Motech's accessible, domain-neutral React interface system. Version 3 is a clean break from the legacy library: use only the components documented in this Storybook and import them from the public package entry points.

## Choose the right layer

| Layer            | Responsibility                                                            | Examples                                                                      |
| ---------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Primitive        | One focused, reusable interface responsibility                            | `Button`, `TextField`, `Stack`, `Dialog`                                      |
| Pattern          | A Breeze-owned composition for a recurring, domain-neutral interface need | `PageHeader`, `Pagination`, `FileUpload`                                      |
| Domain component | An application-owned composition that represents a business concept       | A project summary or account record built from Breeze primitives and patterns |

Applications own domain components, business copy, data fetching, persistence, routing decisions and business validation. Breeze owns its visual language, accessible interaction behaviour and documented public contracts.

## Public entry points

```tsx
import {
  BreezeProvider,
  Button,
  Stack,
  Typography,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { AddIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';
import '@motech-development/breeze-ui/reset.css';
import '@motech-development/breeze-ui/styles.css';
```

Do not use internal deep imports. React Aria, Lucide and internal styling helpers are implementation details rather than public API.

## Core conventions

- Wrap every Breeze subtree in `BreezeProvider` and provide a BCP 47 `locale`.
- Import `styles.css` once. Greenfield applications can import `reset.css` first.
- Use `onAction` for discrete activation, `onChange` for semantic values, and `onOpenChange` for overlay state.
- Use `value` with `onChange` for controlled state and `defaultValue` for uncontrolled state.
- Multi-part controls expose one compound namespace, such as `Dialog.Root`, `Dialog.Trigger` and `Dialog.Content`.
- Use typed variants and `className` for placement and composition; internal class names are not a stable contract.

## Start here

Read [Installation](?path=/docs/guides-02-installation--docs), then [Quick start](?path=/docs/guides-03-quick-start--docs). The remaining guides explain provider setup, composition, state, accessibility, internationalisation, routing, styling, collections and icons. Each component page contains a public import, deliberately authored examples, anatomy, API tables and selection guidance.
guides-01-overview--docs.summary: What Breeze UI v3 provides, how its primitives and patterns fit together, and where to begin.

guides-02-installation--docs.name: Docs
guides-02-installation--docs.title: Guides/02 Installation
guides-02-installation--docs.content:
import { Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';

<Meta
  title="Guides/02 Installation"
  summary="Install Breeze UI v3, load its published CSS, and satisfy its React peer dependencies."
/>

# Installation

Install the package with your application's package manager. Breeze UI v3 requires React 19 and React DOM 19 as peer dependencies.

```shell
yarn add @motech-development/breeze-ui react react-dom
```

## Load the styles

Every application must load the compiled component stylesheet once:

```tsx
import '@motech-development/breeze-ui/styles.css';
```

Greenfield applications may opt into the Breeze global baseline by importing the reset before the component stylesheet:

```tsx
import '@motech-development/breeze-ui/reset.css';
import '@motech-development/breeze-ui/styles.css';
```

`styles.css` contains fonts, semantic tokens, component-scoped normalisation and component styles. `reset.css` contains the optional global document baseline. Existing applications should assess the global reset before enabling it.

## Add the provider

All Breeze components require `BreezeProvider`:

```tsx
import { BreezeProvider } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function App() {
  return <BreezeProvider locale="en-GB">Your application</BreezeProvider>;
}
```

Use a valid BCP 47 locale. Add `timeZone`, `router`, `messages`, `direction` or a custom `portalContainer` only when the application needs those environment behaviours.

## Package boundaries

| Import                                     | Purpose                                        |
| ------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------- |
| `@motech-development/breeze-ui`            | Components, patterns and public types          |
| `@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons`      | The 32 curated icon components and `IconProps` |
| `@motech-development/breeze-ui/styles.css` | Required compiled styles                       |
| `@motech-development/breeze-ui/reset.css`  | Optional global reset                          |

Do not configure Tailwind to scan Breeze source, import unpublished CSS files or depend on `react-aria-components` through Breeze. The package's public entry points are the supported boundary.
guides-02-installation--docs.summary: Install Breeze UI v3, load its published CSS, and satisfy its React peer dependencies.

guides-03-quick-start--docs.name: Docs
guides-03-quick-start--docs.title: Guides/03 Quick start
guides-03-quick-start--docs.content:
import { Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';

<Meta
  title="Guides/03 Quick start"
  summary="Build a small accessible Breeze interface using the provider, layout, typography, fields and actions."
/>

# Quick start

This example uses only the public v3 API and domain-neutral copy. The field manages its own initial value, while form submission remains application-owned.

```tsx
import {
  BreezeProvider,
  Button,
  FormActions,
  Stack,
  Surface,
  TextField,
  Typography,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import '@motech-development/breeze-ui/reset.css';
import '@motech-development/breeze-ui/styles.css';

export function App() {
  return (
    <BreezeProvider locale="en-GB" timeZone="Europe/London">
      <Surface padding="lg">
        <form
          onSubmit={(event) => {
            event.preventDefault();
          }}
        >
          <Stack gap="md">
            <Typography as="h1">Create an item</Typography>

            <TextField.Root name="title" defaultValue="Draft item" required>
              <TextField.Label>Title</TextField.Label>
              <TextField.Input />
              <TextField.Description>
                Use a short, recognisable title.
              </TextField.Description>
              <TextField.Error>Enter a title.</TextField.Error>
            </TextField.Root>

            <FormActions primary={<Button type="submit">Save item</Button>} />
          </Stack>
        </form>
      </Surface>
    </BreezeProvider>
  );
}
```

## Why this composition

- `BreezeProvider` supplies locale, direction, messages, routing and overlay infrastructure.
- `Surface` provides a themed visual boundary; `Stack` owns vertical spacing.
- `Typography` separates semantic heading choice from visual styling.
- `TextField` keeps its label, control, description and error in one accessible compound field.
- `FormActions` provides the recurring form-action layout without taking ownership of submission.
- `Button` performs an action. Use `Link` or `LinkButton` when activation navigates.

## Next steps

Read [Provider and styles](?path=/docs/guides-04-provider-and-styles--docs) before integrating routing or localisation. Use the individual component pages to choose variants, understand defaults and copy deliberately authored examples.
guides-03-quick-start--docs.summary: Build a small accessible Breeze interface using the provider, layout, typography, fields and actions.

guides-04-provider-and-styles--docs.name: Docs
guides-04-provider-and-styles--docs.title: Guides/04 Provider and styles
guides-04-provider-and-styles--docs.content:
import { Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';

<Meta
  title="Guides/04 Provider and styles"
  summary="Configure the required BreezeProvider boundary, published styles, portals, messages and toast capacity."
/>

# Provider and styles

`BreezeProvider` is mandatory. It establishes one environment for locale-sensitive behaviour, reading direction, generic messages, router adaptation, overlays, portals and toasts.

```tsx
import { BreezeProvider } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import '@motech-development/breeze-ui/styles.css';

<BreezeProvider locale="en-GB" timeZone="Europe/London" toastLimit={3}>
  <App />
</BreezeProvider>;
```

## Provider API

| Prop              | Type                      | Default                    | Required | Purpose                                                    |
| ----------------- | ------------------------- | -------------------------- | -------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
| `children`        | `ReactNode`               | —                          | Yes      | Breeze components and application content                  |
| `locale`          | `string`                  | —                          | Yes      | BCP 47 locale for formatting and interaction               |
| `direction`       | `'ltr' \| 'rtl'`          | Inferred from `locale`     | No       | Explicit reading-direction override                        |
| `messages`        | `Partial<BreezeMessages>` | Built-in `en-GB` catalogue | No       | Generic Breeze labels and announcements                    |
| `timeZone`        | `string`                  | Browser/runtime context    | No       | IANA time-zone name for formatting                         |
| `router`          | `BreezeRouterAdapter`     | Native navigation          | No       | Client-side navigation for eligible local URLs             |
| `portalContainer` | `HTMLElement \| null`     | Provider-owned body portal | No       | Host for dialogs, popovers, menus, tooltips and toasts     |
| `toastLimit`      | `number`                  | `3`                        | No       | Positive integer maximum of visible provider-scoped toasts |

An invalid `toastLimit` throws a `RangeError`. A supplied portal container receives Breeze scope, language and direction attributes while mounted. Keep nested providers intentional: each provider owns its own environment and toast queue.

## Message catalogue

The replaceable generic keys are `clear`, `close`, `loading`, `noResults`, `notifications`, `nextPage`, `page`, `previousPage`, `selectDate`, `selectDateTime`, `selectTime`, `hidePassword`, `showPassword` and `unreadNotifications`. Domain and product copy still belongs in component props or children.

```tsx
<BreezeProvider
  locale="en-GB"
  messages={{
    clear: 'Clear value',
    notifications: 'Updates',
  }}
>
  <App />
</BreezeProvider>
```

## CSS contract

Use `styles.css` in every application. Import `reset.css` first only when the application opts into the global baseline. Supported visual differences come from typed component props. `className` is for placement and composition; do not target internal selectors, depend on generated utility classes or replace semantic tokens as a component API.
guides-04-provider-and-styles--docs.summary: Configure the required BreezeProvider boundary, published styles, portals, messages and toast capacity.

guides-05-composition-and-compound-compo.name: Docs
guides-05-composition-and-compound-compo.title: Guides/05 Composition and compound components
guides-05-composition-and-compound-compo.content:
import { Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';

<Meta
  title="Guides/05 Composition and compound components"
  summary="Compose Breeze primitives and use every public part of a compound component without depending on internal DOM."
/>

# Composition and compound components

Simple primitives such as `Button` and `Surface` expose one component. Multi-part controls expose a Breeze-owned namespace so each semantic element can receive its own content, native attributes, ref and `className`.

## Read compound anatomy from the namespace

```tsx
import { Button, Dialog } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<Dialog.Root>
  <Dialog.Trigger>Open details</Dialog.Trigger>
  <Dialog.Content>
    <Dialog.Title>Item details</Dialog.Title>
    <Dialog.Description>
      Review the information before continuing.
    </Dialog.Description>
    <Dialog.Close>Close</Dialog.Close>
    <Button onAction={() => saveItem()}>Save</Button>
  </Dialog.Content>
</Dialog.Root>;
```

The root coordinates shared state and behaviour. Triggers perform the opening action. Content owns the layered surface and focus scope. Title and description provide the accessible name and description. Close performs semantic dismissal. The exact rendered DOM is not a public composition contract.

## Composition rules

- Keep all parts inside their matching root unless the component page explicitly says otherwise.
- Include every required semantic part. A visual placeholder is not a label.
- Pass interaction state to the root; pass presentation or native attributes to the part that renders them.
- Use children for application copy and content. Breeze patterns never hard-code product language.
- Use `className` for layout placement and application composition, not to replace supported variants.
- Do not use an `asChild`, universal `as`, slot-class map or internal React Aria component. Breeze exposes constrained semantic choices only where they are supported.

## Primitives, patterns and domain components

Use primitives for focused responsibilities and patterns for recurring, domain-neutral compositions. Build application-owned domain components from both:

```tsx
import {
  Badge,
  Card,
  Inline,
  Stack,
  Typography,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function ItemSummary() {
  return (
    <Card.Root>
      <Card.Header>
        <Inline gap="sm">
          <Card.Title>Draft item</Card.Title>
          <Badge>Draft</Badge>
        </Inline>
        <Card.Description>Updated today</Card.Description>
      </Card.Header>
      <Card.Body>
        <Stack gap="sm">
          <Typography>Application-owned information.</Typography>
        </Stack>
      </Card.Body>
    </Card.Root>
  );
}
```

`ItemSummary` is a domain component because its meaning belongs to the application. It should stay outside Breeze even though it is composed entirely from Breeze parts.

## Discoverability

Each compound component page documents its root and every public part in one anatomy and API reference. Story metadata lists public `subcomponents` so the component manifest carries the same structure.
guides-05-composition-and-compound-compo.summary: Compose Breeze primitives and use every public part of a compound component without depending on internal DOM.

guides-06-state-and-forms--docs.name: Docs
guides-06-state-and-forms--docs.title: Guides/06 State and forms
guides-06-state-and-forms--docs.content:
import { Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';

<Meta
  title="Guides/06 State and forms"
  summary="Use Breeze controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, validation and native form contracts consistently."
/>

# State and forms

Breeze state callbacks receive semantic values rather than DOM events. Applications own form libraries, schemas, submission, cross-field rules, server errors and business validation.

## State vocabulary

| Behaviour            | Controlled              | Uncontrolled       | Callback                           |
| -------------------- | ----------------------- | ------------------ | ---------------------------------- |
| Field value          | `value`                 | `defaultValue`     | `onChange(nextValue)`              |
| Open state           | `open`                  | `defaultOpen`      | `onOpenChange(nextOpen)`           |
| Collection selection | `selection`             | `defaultSelection` | `onSelectionChange(nextSelection)` |
| Toggle state         | `selected`              | `defaultSelected`  | `onChange(nextSelected)`           |
| Discrete activation  | Application-owned state | —                  | `onAction()`                       |

Controlled and uncontrolled props are mutually exclusive TypeScript unions. A mutable controlled value requires its change callback. To display an intentionally immutable controlled value, use `readOnly` where the API supports it.

```tsx
import { TextField } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { useState } from 'react';

export function ControlledTitleField() {
  const [title, setTitle] = useState('Draft item');

  return (
    <TextField.Root value={title} onChange={setTitle} required>
      <TextField.Label>Title</TextField.Label>
      <TextField.Input name="title" />
      <TextField.Description>Use a concise title.</TextField.Description>
      <TextField.Error>Enter a title.</TextField.Error>
    </TextField.Root>
  );
}
```

```tsx
<TextField.Root value="Archived item" readOnly>
  <TextField.Label>Title</TextField.Label>
  <TextField.Input name="title" />
</TextField.Root>
```

## Validation and errors

- `required` marks native and accessible required state; it does not invent application validation rules.
- `invalid` exposes invalid state and reveals the component's error part where applicable.
- Place persistent guidance in `Description` and validation feedback in `Error`.
- Keep labels visible. Placeholder text is an example or hint, never the only accessible name.
- Disabled controls do not accept interaction; explain why an unavailable action is disabled in nearby text when that reason is not evident.
- Loading and option-loading errors are distinct from a selected value or field validation error. Use the dedicated parts provided by collection fields.

## Native forms

Use documented `name`, `type`, `value`, `required`, `autoComplete` and submission attributes on the relevant control part. Native attributes that Breeze deliberately omits are not supported through an escape hatch. Breeze does not provide a `Form` component, schema adapter, field registration API, mask language or domain-specific field.

## Callback semantics

`onAction` means the user activated a discrete action; it does not expose a mouse or press event. `onChange` reports the next public value. Selection callbacks report `CollectionSelection`, which is a list of string or number keys or the literal `'all'`. Date and time callbacks report the ISO string contracts described in the internationalisation guide.
guides-06-state-and-forms--docs.summary: Use Breeze controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, validation and native form contracts consistently.

guides-07-accessibility--docs.name: Docs
guides-07-accessibility--docs.title: Guides/07 Accessibility
guides-07-accessibility--docs.content:
import { Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';

<Meta
  title="Guides/07 Accessibility"
  summary="Meet Breeze consumer responsibilities for names, descriptions, keyboard use, focus, status and WCAG 2.2 AA content."
/>

# Accessibility

Breeze targets WCAG 2.2 AA and the relevant WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices. Components supply semantics, focus behaviour and keyboard interaction; applications must supply meaningful names, content, relationships and workflow decisions.

## Consumer checklist

- Give every interactive component an accessible name through its documented label, visible text, `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby` contract.
- Use visible `Label`, `Legend`, `Title` and `Description` parts where the compound API provides them.
- Keep heading levels and landmarks in a logical application hierarchy.
- Use `IconButton` with a specific accessible label. Hide decorative icons from assistive technology.
- Do not communicate status, validation or selection through colour alone.
- Preserve the visible Breeze focus indicator and sufficient contrast when composing with `className`.
- Use `VisuallyHidden` for essential text that should remain available to assistive technology, not to conceal interactive controls.
- Put `SkipLink` first in the focus order and point it at a stable main-content ID.

## Keyboard behaviour

Native buttons activate with <kbd>Enter</kbd> and <kbd>Space</kbd>; links activate with <kbd>Enter</kbd>. Compound widgets implement the established pattern for their role, including arrow-key movement, <kbd>Home</kbd>/<kbd>End</kbd> where applicable, and <kbd>Escape</kbd> dismissal for overlays. Read the component page for its exact orientation and selection behaviour.

Do not add competing keyboard handlers to internal parts. Application shortcuts must not prevent text entry, expected browser commands or the documented widget interaction.

## Overlays and focus

Dialogs and alert dialogs move focus into their content, contain focus while modal, close through their documented controls and restore focus appropriately. Popovers, menus and tooltips have different modality and dismissal rules; do not substitute one merely because the visual treatment looks similar.

Every dialog-like surface needs a meaningful title. Descriptions should explain consequence or context rather than repeat the title. Destructive confirmation belongs in `AlertDialog` or `ConfirmationDialog`, not a generic `Dialog`.

## Dynamic content

Use `Alert` for important inline status, `Toast` for transient notifications, and progress components for ongoing work. Supply visible text for indeterminate progress and errors. Avoid repeatedly announcing decorative loading placeholders; `Skeleton` is visual and must be paired with an appropriately labelled busy region when the loading state matters.

## Testing the application composition

Test through roles, names, labels, text, keyboard interaction and public state. Do not assert internal React Aria structure, generated classes or private data attributes. Automated checks complement, but do not replace, keyboard and screen-reader review of complete workflows.
guides-07-accessibility--docs.summary: Meet Breeze consumer responsibilities for names, descriptions, keyboard use, focus, status and WCAG 2.2 AA content.

guides-08-internationalization-dates-tim.name: Docs
guides-08-internationalization-dates-tim.title: Guides/08 Internationalization, dates, time zones, and direction
guides-08-internationalization-dates-tim.content:
import { Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';

<Meta
  title="Guides/08 Internationalization, dates, time zones, and direction"
  summary="Configure locale, translations, ISO date and time values, IANA time zones and right-to-left direction."
/>

# Internationalization, dates, time zones, and direction

Set a BCP 47 locale on `BreezeProvider`. Breeze uses it for `Intl` formatting, date and collection behaviour, accessible announcements and inferred reading direction.

```tsx
import { BreezeProvider } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<BreezeProvider
  locale="en-GB"
  timeZone="Europe/London"
  messages={{
    clear: 'Clear value',
    noResults: 'No matching options',
  }}
>
  <App />
</BreezeProvider>;
```

## Public temporal values

| Value       | Public format                                         | Example                                      |
| ----------- | ----------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Date        | `YYYY-MM-DD`                                          | `'2026-07-20'`                               |
| Time        | `HH:mm` or `HH:mm:ss`                                 | `'14:30'` or `'14:30:45'`                    |
| Date range  | `{ start: string; end: string }` with inclusive dates | `{ start: '2026-07-20', end: '2026-07-24' }` |
| Date-time   | ISO 8601 with an explicit offset                      | `'2026-07-20T14:30:00+01:00'`                |
| Empty field | `null`                                                | `null`                                       |

Use these strings at the application boundary. Do not import or pass `@internationalized/date` objects or React Aria value types; those are internal.

## Time zones

`timeZone` is an IANA name such as `Europe/London` or `America/New_York`. It supplies shared display context, but a date-time value still carries an explicit offset so it is unambiguous. A plain date has no instant or time zone. A plain time has no date or zone. Choose the component that matches the meaning of the value rather than converting everything to a timestamp.

## Messages and product copy

The provider's partial `messages` catalogue replaces Breeze-owned generic labels and announcements. Map the application's translation system into this object. Visible domain or product copy belongs in component children and props; Breeze does not translate it automatically.

## Direction

Breeze infers `ltr` or `rtl` from the locale and applies `dir` and `lang` to both the component root and portal root. Use the explicit `direction` prop only for a real override or focused testing.

```tsx
<BreezeProvider locale="en-GB" direction="rtl">
  <App />
</BreezeProvider>
```

Layout primitives and directional controls use logical direction. Do not reverse child arrays, hard-code left/right margins or replace locale-specific arrow-key behaviour in application code. Test translated copy at realistic lengths and verify both main content and portalled overlays.

## Formatting components

Use `FormattedDateTime`, `FormattedNumber`, `FormattedList` and `RelativeTime` for locale-sensitive display. Use date/time fields, pickers and calendars for interaction. Formatting components display values; they do not edit, parse business input or own persistence.
guides-08-internationalization-dates-tim.summary: Configure locale, translations, ISO date and time values, IANA time zones and right-to-left direction.

guides-09-routing--docs.name: Docs
guides-09-routing--docs.title: Guides/09 Routing
guides-09-routing--docs.summary: Connect a router-neutral navigation adapter while preserving anchors, external URLs, downloads and modified clicks.

guides-10-styling-and-responsive-layout-.name: Docs
guides-10-styling-and-responsive-layout-.title: Guides/10 Styling and responsive layout
guides-10-styling-and-responsive-layout-.content:
import { Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';

<Meta
  title="Guides/10 Styling and responsive layout"
  summary="Use compiled Breeze styles, semantic variants, bounded spacing and mobile-first responsive layout values."
/>

# Styling and responsive layout

Breeze publishes one visual theme. Components expose typed visual choices and layout primitives expose bounded responsive values; applications should not depend on internal Tailwind classes or DOM structure.

## Choose a layout primitive

| Need                                                   | Component   |
| ------------------------------------------------------ | ----------- |
| Constrain page width and horizontal gutters            | `Container` |
| Vertical flow                                          | `Stack`     |
| Horizontal flow that may wrap                          | `Inline`    |
| Bounded columns or a documented unequal-track form row | `Grid`      |
| Centre one child                                       | `Center`    |
| Themed background, border and inset                    | `Surface`   |
| Semantic visual division                               | `Separator` |

Keep authored DOM order meaningful. Responsive layout changes visual arrangement without rewriting reading or focus order.

## Responsive values

Responsive layout props accept either a scalar or a mobile-first object with a required `base` value. Breakpoints are `sm` at 681px, `md` at 901px and `lg` at 1181px.

```tsx
import { Grid, Stack, Surface } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<Surface padding={{ base: 'md', md: 'lg', lg: 'xl' }}>
  <Grid columns={{ base: 1, sm: 2, lg: 4 }} gap="lg">
    <Stack gap="sm">First</Stack>
    <Stack gap="sm">Second</Stack>
    <Stack gap="sm">Third</Stack>
    <Stack gap="sm">Fourth</Stack>
  </Grid>
</Surface>;
```

Omitted breakpoint keys inherit the preceding value. Supported spacing names are `none`, `xs`, `sm`, `control`, `compact`, `md`, `lg`, `xl`, `xxl` and `page`. Use `control` and `compact` only where their named role matches the composition.

## Visual vocabulary

Components expose only the values that apply to them. Shared vocabulary includes `variant` for semantic colour, `appearance` for emphasis, `size` for control scale, `density` for information spacing and `orientation` for interaction/layout axis. Always check the component's API table rather than assuming every shared value is accepted.

## `className`

Use `className` for placement and application composition—for example, assigning a grid area or constraining a component in an application layout. Prefer public component props for colour, padding, size, tone and state. Do not target descendant selectors, generated data attributes, utility-class order or private CSS variables as stable API.

## CSS loading

Import `@motech-development/breeze-ui/styles.css` once. Greenfield applications may import `@motech-development/breeze-ui/reset.css` first. Consumers do not need Tailwind, do not scan Breeze source and should not reproduce Breeze recipes in application CSS.
guides-10-styling-and-responsive-layout-.summary: Use compiled Breeze styles, semantic variants, bounded spacing and mobile-first responsive layout values.

guides-11-collections-and-virtualization.name: Docs
guides-11-collections-and-virtualization.title: Guides/11 Collections and virtualization
guides-11-collections-and-virtualization.content:
import { Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';

<Meta
  title="Guides/11 Collections and virtualization"
  summary="Choose a Breeze collection, supply stable keys, model selection and configure optional fixed or variable virtualization."
/>

# Collections and virtualization

Breeze collection primitives share stable string-or-number keys, static compound children and typed `items` render functions. Applications own fetching, cursors, caching, sorting, filtering, grouping decisions and page merging.

## Choose a collection

| Need                                      | Component                   |
| ----------------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| Plain semantic content without selection  | `List` or `DescriptionList` |
| Visible selectable options                | `ListBox`                   |
| Selectable rows that may contain controls | `GridList`                  |
| Structured rows and columns with headings | `Table`                     |
| Compact popup choice                      | `Select`                    |
| Searchable or custom-value choice         | `ComboBox`                  |
| Commands and nested actions               | `Menu`                      |

Use `TagGroup` for a compact editable set of tags, not as a replacement for a general collection.

## Static and data-driven content

```tsx
import { ListBox } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<ListBox.Root aria-label="Priority" defaultSelection={['normal']}>
  <ListBox.Item id="low" textValue="Low">
    Low
  </ListBox.Item>
  <ListBox.Item id="normal" textValue="Normal">
    Normal
  </ListBox.Item>
  <ListBox.Item id="high" textValue="High">
    High
  </ListBox.Item>
</ListBox.Root>;
```

```tsx
const priorities = [
  { id: 'low', label: 'Low' },
  { id: 'normal', label: 'Normal' },
  { id: 'high', label: 'High' },
];

<ListBox.Root aria-label="Priority" items={priorities}>
  {(item) => (
    <ListBox.Item id={item.id} textValue={item.label}>
      {item.label}
    </ListBox.Item>
  )}
</ListBox.Root>;
```

Every item needs a stable `id`. `textValue` supplies typeahead and accessible text when the rendered content is not plain text. Selection is `CollectionKey[] | 'all'`; use `selection` plus `onSelectionChange` for controlled state, `defaultSelection` for uncontrolled state, and `readOnly` for an intentionally immutable controlled selection.

## Virtualization

`Table`, `GridList` and `ListBox` accept a Breeze-owned `virtualization` configuration. No `Virtualizer`, `DataTable` or `VirtualizedTable` component is exported.

```tsx
<ListBox.Root
  aria-label="Items"
  items={items}
  virtualization={{
    mode: 'fixed',
    rowHeight: 44,
    viewportHeight: 320,
    overscan: 88,
  }}
>
  {(item) => (
    <ListBox.Item id={item.id} textValue={item.label}>
      {item.label}
    </ListBox.Item>
  )}
</ListBox.Root>
```

| Option                   | Required               | Default                      | Meaning                                        |
| ------------------------ | ---------------------- | ---------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| `mode`                   | Yes                    | —                            | `'fixed'` or `'variable'`                      |
| `viewportHeight`         | Yes                    | —                            | Fixed scroll viewport height in pixels         |
| `rowHeight`              | Fixed mode             | —                            | Authoritative uniform row height in pixels     |
| `estimatedRowHeight`     | No, variable mode only | `48`                         | Initial estimate replaced by live measurements |
| `gap`                    | No                     | `0`                          | Pixel gap between measured rows                |
| `overscan`               | No                     | `0` beyond the engine buffer | Additional retained pixels around the viewport |
| `loadingRowHeight`       | No                     | `48`                         | Initial loading-row height                     |
| `estimatedSectionHeight` | No                     | Component estimate           | Initial section header/footer estimate         |

Grid-list virtualization additionally accepts `minimumItemWidth` (default `224`) and `maximumColumns`. Keep item height consistent with fixed mode; choose variable mode when content can wrap or expand.

## Loading and empty states

Use `emptyContent` for a genuine empty result and the component's `LoadMore` part for incremental loading. `onLoadMore` requests more application-owned items; `loading` suppresses duplicate requests. Do not place fetching, cursor or cache state inside a render function. Keep focusable keys stable while pages are merged so keyboard focus and selection survive window changes.
guides-11-collections-and-virtualization.summary: Choose a Breeze collection, supply stable keys, model selection and configure optional fixed or variable virtualization.

guides-12-icons--docs.name: Docs
guides-12-icons--docs.title: Guides/12 Icons
guides-12-icons--docs.content:
import { Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';

<Meta
  title="Guides/12 Icons"
  summary="Choose, import, size and label Breeze's curated icon exports without coupling applications to Lucide."
/>

# Icons

Breeze exposes a curated icon set through `@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons`. Import named icons from that entry point; do not import Lucide through Breeze or use internal icon paths.

```tsx
import {
  AddIcon,
  ExternalLinkIcon,
  WarningIcon,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';
```

## Sizing and colour

Every icon accepts `size?: number | string` and `strokeWidth?: number`. `size` defaults to `1em`, so an icon follows the surrounding text scale. A number is interpreted as CSS pixels; a string may be any valid CSS length. Icons use `currentColor`, allowing the containing Breeze component or text colour to remain authoritative.

```tsx
<AddIcon />
<AddIcon size={20} />
<AddIcon size="1.5rem" strokeWidth={1.75} />
```

Use component `size` props and the default `1em` behaviour before assigning isolated icon dimensions. Do not use an icon's geometry as a spacing system.

## Decorative and meaningful icons

Icons without `aria-label` are decorative: Breeze applies `aria-hidden="true"` and `focusable="false"`. This is correct when visible text or the containing control already provides the accessible name.

```tsx
import { Button } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { AddIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';

<Button>
  <AddIcon />
  Add item
</Button>;
```

When a standalone icon conveys information that is not present in adjacent text, provide a concise `aria-label`; Breeze then exposes it as an image. For icon-only actions, prefer `IconButton` and label the button rather than the icon.

```tsx
<WarningIcon aria-label="Warning" />
```

Do not use an icon as the only indication of status, error or selection. Pair meaning with text or an accessible control state.

## Catalogue and selection

The attached [Icons catalogue](?path=/docs/foundation-icons--docs) literally documents all 32 exports and the intended meaning of each. Reuse the closest curated semantic icon. If no icon fits, first reconsider whether text is clearer; expanding the public icon catalogue is a library decision rather than an application deep-import workaround.
guides-12-icons--docs.summary: Choose, import, size and label Breeze's curated icon exports without coupling applications to Lucide.
